coredata.dc:title: One-step fabrication of wavelength-tunable luminescence of gold-silver bimetallic nanoclusters: Robust performance for α-glucosidase assay
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mengjun
Zhou, Xiaobin
Wang, Xiyang
Wang, Mengke
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, wavelength-tunable gold/silver bimetallic nanoclusters (Au/Ag NCs) were firstly prepared by using 5-methyl-2-thiouracil (5-MTU) as the reducing and capping agent. Notably, we found that regulating the concentration of 5-MTU could change the emission peak position of Au/Ag NCs. Green-emitting nanoclusters (Au/Ag (g) NCs) were achieved at a relatively low concentration of 5-MTU, whereas continuously increasing the 5-MTU concentration was beneficial to the formation of nanoclusters with intense red-emission (Au/Ag (r) NCs). Owing to the excellent water-solubility and superior stability of the Au/Ag (r) NCs, a sensitive ratiometric ﬂuorescent platform for quantitative analysis of α-glucosidase was developed. In this strategy, a glycine-NaOH buffer was ingeniously adopted to trigger the dopamine (DA) self-polymerize process, which could rapidly generate polydopamine (PDA) with bright fluorescence at 470 nm and efficaciously quench the fluorescence of Au/Ag (r) NCs at 600 nm through photoinduced electron-transfer (PET) effect. By measuring the emission ratio F600/F470, this “off-on-off” sensing strategy exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity toward α-glucosidase ranging from 0.01 to 0.5 U mL−1 with quite a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.007 U mL-1. Additionally, this Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based ﬂuorometric system was utilized for α-glucosidase assay in serum samples with impressively results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetallic nanoclusters
Fluorescence
Fluorescence detection
Polydopamine
α-Glucosidase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the wavelength-tunable Au/Ag NCs
Fabrication and characterization of Au/Ag NCs
Construction of Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based ratiometric sensing platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775052 and No. 21575048 ), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption, photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of Au/Ag (g) NCs. Inset: their images under (left) ambient light and (right) UV light. (b) UV–vis spectra of 5-MTU. (c) Absorption, photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of Au/Ag (r) NCs. Inset: their images under (left) ambient light and (right) UV light. Fluorescence intensity of the Au/Ag (g) NCs (d) and Au/Ag (r) NCs (e) under different excitations. (f) FT-IR spectra of 5-MTU, Au/Ag (g) NCs and Au/Ag (r) NCs.

(a) TEM images of Au/Ag (g) NCs. Inset: their size-distribution histogram. (b) TEM images of Au/Ag (r) NCs. Inset: their size-distribution histogram. (c) HRTEM micrographs of the Au/Ag (g) NCs. (d) HRTEM micrographs of the Au/Ag (r) NCs.

XPS Au 4f spectra of Au/Ag (g) NCs (a) and Au/Ag (r) NCs (b). (c) Shifting of XPS Au 4f data in Au/Ag (g) NCs and Au/Ag (r) NCs. XPS Ag 3d spectra of Au/Ag (g) NCs (d) and Au/Ag (r) NCs (e). (f) Shifting of XPS Ag 3d data in Au/Ag (g) NCs and Au/Ag (r) NCs.

(a) Feasibility of the designed Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based biosensor. (b) Emission spectra of Au/Ag (r) NCs toward various DA concentrations (0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0,4, 0.6, 0.8 mmol L−1). Inset exhibited the effect of DPA concentration on the emission ratio F600/F470 of Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA system. The error bars were obtained from three parallel test results. (c) UV–vis absorption curves with various concentrations of DA. (d) Fluorescence lifetime trajectories of Au/Ag (r) NCs towards various DA concentrations.

(a) Emission spectra of the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based system responding to various amounts of α-glucosidase (0, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 0.75 U mL−1). Inset: linearity of F600/F470 vs the concentration of α-glucosidase. The error bars were obtained from three parallel test results. (b) Inhibition efficiency upon the addition of acarbose. The error bars were obtained from three parallel test results. (c) Selectivity of the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA system for α-glucosidase analysis. The error bars were obtained from three parallel test results. (d) Ratio fluorescent responses to their mixture with α-glucosidase. The error bars were obtained from three parallel test results. (Conditions: 0.2 U mL-1 α-glucosidase, 2 U L-1 other bio-enzymes, and 50 μmol L-1 biomolecules).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis route of luminescent bimetallic nanoclusters (a) and the ratiometric fluorescent strategy for α-glucosidase assay with the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA composite (b).
Analysis of α-glucosidase in serum samples.

head.ce:title: One-step fabrication of wavelength-tunable luminescence of gold-silver bimetallic nanoclusters: Robust performance for α-glucosidase assay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, wavelength-tunable gold/silver bimetallic nanoclusters (Au/Ag NCs) were firstly prepared by using 5-methyl-2-thiouracil (5-MTU) as the reducing and capping agent. Notably, we found that regulating the concentration of 5-MTU could change the emission peak position of Au/Ag NCs. Green-emitting nanoclusters (Au/Ag (g) NCs) were achieved at a relatively low concentration of 5-MTU, whereas continuously increasing the 5-MTU concentration was beneficial to the formation of nanoclusters with intense red-emission (Au/Ag (r) NCs). Owing to the excellent water-solubility and superior stability of the Au/Ag (r) NCs, a sensitive ratiometric ﬂuorescent platform for quantitative analysis of α-glucosidase was developed. In this strategy, a glycine-NaOH buffer was ingeniously adopted to trigger the dopamine (DA) self-polymerize process, which could rapidly generate polydopamine (PDA) with bright fluorescence at 470 nm and efficaciously quench the fluorescence of Au/Ag (r) NCs at 600 nm through photoinduced electron-transfer (PET) effect. By measuring the emission ratio F600/F470, this “off-on-off” sensing strategy exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity toward α-glucosidase ranging from 0.01 to 0.5 U mL−1 with quite a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.007 U mL-1. Additionally, this Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based ﬂuorometric system was utilized for α-glucosidase assay in serum samples with impressively results.

ce:section.ce:para: Noble metal nanoclusters (NCs), a key bridge between atom and nanoparticle (NP), have been comprehensively studied on account of their fascinating properties, such as intriguing optical properties, superior biocompatibility and excellent catalytic activities [1–4]. As emerging and promising nanomaterials, these metal NCs stabilized by various stabilizing agents have been extensively applicated in various fields, ranging from catalysis to bioanalysis [5–7]. The uniform and stable NCs are usually achieved via heating and stirring method [8,9], microwave-assisted approach [10], hydrothermal method [11,12], and used multitudinous capping agents, such as thiols [13], polymers [14], DNAs [15], proteins and peptides [16,17]. Many researches focused on tunning the properties of monometallic nanoclusters via changing the number of atoms [18] or using different ligands [19]. Bimetallic nanoclusters with tunability of composition provide an additional degree of freedom. For example, Negishi et al. revealed that Ag doping strongly affected the electronic structure and the luminescence properties of Au25(SC12H25)18 [20]. Zheng et al. have designed DNA-Ag/Pt nanoclusters with highly eﬀ ;ective peroxidase-like activity, which can be utilized to fabricate a label-free colorimetric apt-sensor for the determination of thrombin [21]. However, the design and synthesis of bimetallic nanoclusters with excellent luminescence properties as well as good practical applicability are still challenging.

Diabetes mellitus (DM) is one of the most common and serious chronic diseases with high incidence around the world. α-Glucosidase is a carbohydrate-hydrolase located at small intestinal epithelial cells and it plays a critical role in keeping normal blood sugar levels. Excess α-glucosidase will promote the production of excessive amount of glucose in blood and result in hyperglycemia, diabetes and other diseases [22]. To date, many approaches have been developed for α-glucosidase screening such as electrochemistry [23], HPLC-ESI-TOF/MS method [24], fluorescence [25] and colorimetry [26]. These methods are very significant for α-glucosidase assay, but some of them require extensive expertise, expensive device, and tedious pretreatment steps. Among them, fluorescence-related methods have attracted widespread attention due to their advantages of less sample consumption, easy implementation, and rapid response. Though most of the fluorescence sensors can provide desirable results, these assays utilize single emission changes and may be easily influenced by environmental factors, instrumental parameters, probe concentrations, etc. [27,28]. Fortunately, the ratiometric fluorescence sensors are able to reduce or eliminate these drawbacks of single-signal detection methods to improve the reliability and accuracy for target analysis since ratiometric fluorescence can provide intrinsic self-calibration for signals by simultaneously recording two or more emission bands [29]. For instance, Shi et al. have designed a MnO2 nanosheet (NS)-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for α-glucosidase assay [30]. Cheng et al. have established a ratiometric sensing strategy based on dual-color CDs for monitoring α-glucosidase activity [31]. Therefore, constructing an accurate and efficient sensing platform for monitoring the α-glucosidase activity is of great signiﬁcance.

Herein, wavelength-tunable Au/Ag NCs were synthesized through mixing the Au and Ag precursors with appropriate concentrations of 5-MTU. Au/Ag (g) NCs were obtained with a low concentration of 5-MTU at room temperature, whereas a high concentration of 5-MTU led to a stable and bright luminescent Au/Ag (r) NCs (Scheme 1 a). Taking advantages of their excellent luminescence performance and stability, the Au/Ag (r) NCs were utilized for fabricating ratiometric fluorescence probe by cooperating with polydopamine for α-glucosidase sensing. As depicted in Scheme 1b, red emissive Au/Ag (r) NCs were initially quenched by the self-polymerization product of dopamine under alkaline condition through PET mechanism [32]. However, ascorbic acid (AA) produced in the α-glucosidase-catalyzed hydrolysis of 2-O-alpha-d-glucopyranosyl-l-ascorbic acid (AAG) can alleviate the self-polymerization process of DA, resulting in a reduction of the ﬂuorescence intensity of PDA at 470 nm and enhancement in a fluorescent signal of Au/Ag (r) NCs at 600 nm. In addition, the introduction of acarbose markedly inhibited the α-glucosidase activity, resulting in the reversion of ratiometric fluorescence intensity. The application of enzymatic specificity and ratiometric strategies in one system significantly improved the sensitivity of the designed Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based sensing platform.
Reagents and apparatus were represented in Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the wavelength-tunable Au/Ag NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 320 μl of 25 mmol L−1 HAuCl4 was dispersed in 80 μl of 25 mmol L−1 AgNO3, and the mixed solution was gently stirred for 30 s. Then, different concentrations of 5-MTU (50, 90 mmol L-1) with a total volume of 320 μl and 80 μl NaOH (1.4 mol L-1) were added into the above mixture and continuously stirred in the dark at room temperature for 4 h. At last, the pure Au/Ag NCs were obtained after dialysis, which were stockpiled in refrigerator prior to use.

The quantitative detection of α-glucosidase activity was carried out as follows: Aliquots of 50 μL of α-glucosidase with different amounts were added into 100 μL of AAG (1.5 mmol L−1) solution and incubated at 37 °C. After 40 min, 100 μL of freshly prepared dopamine (8 mmol L−1) solution, 20 μL Au/Ag (r) NCs, and 100 μL glycine-NaOH (5 mmol L−1, pH 10.4) were subsequently introduced and the solution was diluted to 1.0 mL. Ultimately, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were achieved after equilibration for 15 min.

50 μL of acarbose with various concentrations were preincubated with 50 μL of 10 U mL−1 α-glucosidase solution and 100 μL of 1.5 mmol L−1 AAG solution at 37 °C for 40 min. Following this, freshly prepared dopamine (100 μL, 8 mmol L−1) solution, 20 μL Au/Ag (r) NCs, and 100 μL glycine-NaOH (5 mmol L−1, pH 10.4) were subsequently introduced and the solution was diluted to 1.0 mL. Ultimately, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were achieved after equilibration for 15 min.

To verify the application of Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA for α-glucosidase assay in biological samples, healthy human blood samples were acquired from the China-Japan Union Hospital (Changchun) according to the rules of relevant laws and ethics. Real serum samples were gained through centrifugation at 11,250 rpm for 10 min. Following this, the standard addition method was performed by adding different amount of α-glucosidase to the serum sample. In addition, the following procedures for α-glucosidase analysis were performed as mentioned above in part 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of Au/Ag NCs
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Scheme 1a, using 5-MTU as the reducing and capping agent, wavelength-tunable Au/Ag NCs were facilely prepared via a one-pot reaction strategy with an appropriate molar ratio of HAuCl4:AgNO3 at 4:1 (Fig. S1). The green-emitting Au/Ag (g) NCs were obtained at a low concentration of 5-MTU, while Au/Ag (r) NCs were achieved upon increasing the concentration of 5-MTU (Fig. S2). As represented in Fig. 1 (a), the maximum emission peak of Au/Ag (g) NCs was located at 499 nm under the excitation of 435 nm, simultaneously accompanying the appearance of an absorbance peak at 430 nm. The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Au/Ag (g) NCs in aqueous solution was calculated to be 3.0 % employing quinine sulfate as a reference (Fig. S3). Notably, the characteristic absorption peak in the region of 220−400 nm could be attributed to the ligand of 5-MTU (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1(c) presented the maximum excitation and emission peaks of Au/Ag (r) NCs was at 360 and 600 nm, respectively. The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Au/Ag (r) NCs in aqueous solution was calculated to be 3.8 % employing rhodamine 6 G as a reference (Fig. S4). Meanwhile, no shift in the fluorescence emission peak position of the Au/Ag (g) NCs and Au/Ag (r) NCs were observed with various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1d and e), manifesting that both the Au/Ag (g) NCs and Au/Ag (r) NCs exhibited an excitation-independent behavior. This phenomenon also confirmed that the Au/Ag NCs with tunable emission had a relatively homogeneous surface and their emission were originated from the relaxed states rather than scattering effects [33]. Additionally, the ﬂuorescence lifetime of Au/Ag (r) NCs (0.73 μs) was relatively higher than Au/Ag (g) NCs (0.14 μs) due to the low photon turnover rates [34] (Fig. S5), indicating that the Au/Ag (r) NCs could have broader application prospects. To obtain Au/Ag (r) NCs with excellent stability and strong emission, the NaOH concentration was optimized initially, and 0.14 mol L−1 was found to be the optimum (Fig. S6a). Meanwhile, reaction temperature influenced the reaction rate, and the optimal result was acquired when the reaction temperature was 37 ℃ (Fig. S6b). Fig. S6c displayed that the optimal reaction time was 4 h. Therefore, a temperature of 37 °C, a reaction time of 4 h, and 0.14 mol L−1 NaOH were chosen for the preparation of Au/Ag (r) NCs.

To figure out the luminescent mechanisms of the Au/Ag NCs with tunable emission, a series of structural characterizations were carried out. Firstly, the functional groups and chemical bonds on Au/Ag NCs were identified by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The CO and NH in the pyrimidine ring were located at 1593 and 1521 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1f). Moreover, the typical band at 1121 cm−1 was assigned to alkyl carbon [35]. Compared with the precursor 5-MTU, the peak of Au/Ag NCs was found to be enhanced at 1593 cm-1 and decreased at 1625 cm-1, which may be due to the formation of new bonds between metal cores and 5-MTU, leading to alterations in the conjugated structure of 5-MTU. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was applied to characterize the morphological feature and dimensional distribution of the wavelength-tunable Au/Ag NCs. The approximate diameter of Au/Ag (g) NCs was 1.52 nm with good mono-dispersity and spherical nanostructure, which was smaller than that of 1.96 nm Au/Ag (r) NCs (Fig. 2 a and b). It was evident that the size of Au/Ag NCs signiﬁcantly increased with the increasing of 5-MTU content. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) was utilized to characterize the prepared Au/Ag NCs. An interplanar spacing of 0.235 nm corresponding to Au (111) and an interplanar spacing of 0.208 nm corresponding to Ag (200) were simultaneously observed within one Au/Ag (g) NCs (Fig. 2c) [36,37]. An interplanar spacing of 0.236 nm corresponding to Au (111) as well as an interplanar spacing of 0.207 nm corresponding to Ag (200) were simultaneously observed within one Au/Ag (r) NCs (Fig. 2d). Thus, we can preliminarily speculate that the Au/Ag NCs were synthesized successfully.

Moreover, the exact valence state and the relative content distribution of elements in the Au/Ag NCs were measured by XPS analysis. The wavelength-tunable Au/Ag NCs were stabilized by 5-MTU molecules and interacted strongly with them through Au-S bond, as shown in Fig. S7. From Fig. 3 a, we can see that the Au 4f7/2 XPS spectra of Au/Ag (g) NCs exhibited two apparent components: the peak at 88.2 eV for Au+ (50.56 %) and the other at 84.5 eV for Au° (49.44 %). With increasing the concentration of 5-MTU, the content of Au+ in Au/Ag NCs decreased from 50.56 % to 49.34 % and the content of Au° in Au/Ag NCs increased from 49.44%–50.66% (Fig. 3b), implying a quite percentage of Au+ in Au/Ag NCs was converted into Au°. Fig. 3c presented that the Au 4f7/2 peak of Au/Ag (r) NCs changed to lower binding energies compared with Au/Ag (g) NCs, supporting that the proportion of Au+ decreases with increasing the concentration of 5-MTU [38]. Meanwhile, we observed that the binding energies of Ag at 368.0 and 373.9 eV corresponded to 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively, which were identical to those of the Ag (I) and Ag (0) species (Fig. 3d) [39,40]. Notably, the content of Ag+ in Au/Ag NCs reduced from 81.46%–73.60% and the content of Ag° in Au/Ag NCs increased from 18.54 % to 26.40 % (Fig. 3e). The peak position of Ag 3d5/2 shifted from 367.8–368.1 eV (Fig. 3f), manifesting that the amount of Ag+ involved in the formation of Au/Ag NCs increased with the increasement of 5-MTU concentrations. Collectively, these results evidently demonstrated that the core size and ratio of Au and Ag in individual dispersed NCs together influenced the luminescence properties of the Au/Ag NCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based ratiometric sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based detection platform for ratiometric screening of α-glucosidase was illustrated in Scheme 1b and related feasibility analysis was exhibited in Fig. 4 a. In this system, the fluorescence of Au/Ag (r) NCs had negligible variations with the addition of α-glucosidase and AAG. Meanwhile, DA could self-polymerize under alkaline conditions to generate fluorescent PDA with intense blue emission. The formed PDA could spontaneously assemble on the Au/Ag (r) NCs via hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking and effectively quench the fluorescence of Au/Ag (r) NCs through photoinduced electron-transfer (PET) effect [41,42]. While upon the introduction of AAG and α-glucosidase to the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA system, the generated AA could effectively restrain the polymerization process of DA on account of the preferentially affinity between AA and DA via Michael addition reaction [43], leading to the attenuation in the ﬂuorescence intensity of PDA at 470 nm and enhancement in the fluorescent signal of Au/Ag (r) NCs at 600 nm. Nevertheless, in the presence of acarbose, a gradual enhancement on the ﬂuorescence signal was observed owing to the competitive and revisable binding process of α-glucosidase between acarbose and AAG. To obtain a sensitive detection of α-glucosidase, the influence of pH and reaction time of the process of self-polymerization were optimized. As evident from Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of PDA apparently changed with variation in the pH value from 9.4 to 10.6 and exhibited the optimal luminescence at pH 10.4. As illustrated in Fig. S9, the emission ratio F600/F470 of the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA system gradually reduced and reached the equilibrium at 15 min. Furthermore, as manifested in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence of PDA at 470 nm increased while the fluorescence of Au/Ag (r) NCs at 600 nm decreased systematically after treatment with DA. From the inset of Fig. 4b, we can clearly notice that the emission ratio F600/F470 of the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA system decreased rapidly in the DA concentration range of 0.05−0.6 mmol L−1 and then remained invariable when the DA concentration exceeded 0.8 mmol L−1. Consistently, characteristic absorbance of the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA system in the visible region was enhanced with increasing DA concentration, which synchronously accompanied the corresponding color changes from colorless to brown, denoting the formation of fluorescent PDA (Fig. 4c). Thus, pH 10.4, 15 min for the self-polymerization of DA and 0.8 mmol L-1 of DA were selected for constructing Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA fluorescence sensing system.

To further confirm the quenching mechanism of Au/Ag (r) NCs by PDA, TEM and fluorescence lifetime tests of the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA system were carried out. The representative TEM images were shown in Fig. S10 revealed that the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA composite was favorably formed through self-polymerization under alkaline conditions, with an average size, ranges from 110 nm to 164 nm. It is well known that PDA was a superior electron acceptor, the formed Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA composite facilitates the process of photoinduced electron-transfer from Au/Ag (r) NCs to PDA. Then, fluorescence lifetime measurements of the Au/Ag(r)NCs/PDA system in the presence of different amounts of DA were conducted to further check the quenching mechanism. As revealed in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence lifetime of Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA system progressively reduced with the increasement of DA concentrations, implying a dynamic quenching process. Dynamic quenching can be explained that the excited state of fluorophores returns to the ground state by the collision between the quencher and fluorophores with the mechanism of energy transfer or the mechanism of charge transfer [44,45]. Noteworthily, one of the requirements for efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is the enough spectral overlap between the donor emission and acceptor absorption [46]. In this sensing strategy, the fluorescence of PDA increased while the fluorescence of Au/Ag (r) NCs decreased. Besides, there is almost no spectral overlap between the emission spectra of PDA and absorption spectra of Au/Ag(r) NCs (Fig. S11). Therefore, the FRET process between Au/Ag (r) NCs and PDA can be ruled out. Thus, the fluorescence of Au/Ag (r) NCs could efficaciously and substantially quenched through the generation of PDA via PET mechanism, thereby facilitating the construction of ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

To enhance the fluorescence response of the ratiometric probe of Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA to α-glucosidase and improve the sensitivity of this system, some vital experimental factors such as enzyme reaction time and AAG concentration for the enzymatic hydrolysis process were optimized. As presented in Fig. S12a and 12b, enzymatic reaction time of 40 min and the concentration of AAG 150 μmol L−1 were chosen for the detection of α-glucosidase. Under optimal assay conditions, with the increase in α-glucosidase concentration, a remarkable increase of the ﬂuorescence at 470 nm was observed, whereas the fluorescence at 600 nm reduced (Fig. 5 a). The emission ratios F600/F470 were proportional to the content of α-glucosidase from 0.01 to 0.5 U mL−1, the regression equation was F600/F470 = 0.577 + 3.217[α-glucosidase] (R2 = 0.992), and the LOD calculated according to 3SB/b (SB is the sample standard deviation of the blanks, n = 11; b is the slope of linear ﬁtting curve) was 0.007 U mL-1. Compared with previously reported methods, as shown in Table S1, the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based ratiometric fluorescence probe had a comparable or even better performance for α-glucosidase activity determination.

As an effective inhibitor of α-glucosidase, acarbose can competitively capture the active site of α-glucosidase and polysaccharide, effectively slowing down of the hydrolysis process of carbohydrates in small intestine, and consequently regulating the concentration of blood glucose and glycated hemoglobin within a normal range [47]. Thus, the α-glucosidase controlled ratiometric fluorescence response can be inhibited by acarbose, resulting in the changes in fluorescent signal. As expected, the inhibition efficiency would increase with increasing the concentration of acarbose and the corresponding IC50 value (half of the maximal inhibitory effect) was approximately equal to 88.50 μmol L−1 (Fig. 5b). The sensor based on Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA provided a sensitive, rapid, and simple way for α-glucosidase inhibitor screening.

To explore the selectivity of this sensing strategy for α-glucosidase assay, control experiments were carried out using various potential interferences (PPase, ChOx, Pep, Pap, AAOx, ALP, Try, GOx, Gal Ox, Urea, Chol and Mal). Benefiting from the stable fluorescent signal of Au/Ag (r) NCs and the high selectivity originated from the enzyme specific recognition process, the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based fluorescence ratiometric sensor exhibited excellent anti-interference capability toward various biological enzymes, which neither responded to Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based biosensor nor had any interference to α-glucosidase assay (Fig. 5c and d). Besides, the influence of more potential interferences, reductive biological molecules (GSH, Cys and AA) were investigated. Fig. S13 showed that GSH, Cys and AA with concentrations of 100 μmol L−1 exhibited obvious interferences on the emission ratio F600/F470. However, the interferences of GSH and Cys could be effectively eliminated by a special thiol-blocking reagent N-ethylmaleimide (NEM). Because AA was unstable and easily oxidized, and the AA in sample would be depleted during the biological sample treatment process [48]. As exhibited in Fig. S14, NEM and serum sample had no influence on the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based sensing system for α-glucosidase assay and acarbose screening. These results indicated that other possible interfering substances in the serum would not affect the determination of α-glucosidase in real samples. The above results clearly indicated that this Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based fluorescence ratiometric sensor possessed high selectivity to α-glucosidase.

To evaluate the practical performance of this system, standard addition method was employed by the introduction of various amount of α-glucosidase into actual serum sample and then measured by the proposed method. It could be noted that the recoveries of α-glucosidase were 94.80–108.00% and the calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) were 0.41–1.09 % (Table 1 ). These results indicated that the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based ratiometric sensor exhibited high reliability and accuracy for the determination of α-glucosidase.

In summary, we presented a facile strategy for the fabrication of wavelength-tunable Au/Ag NCs by regulating the concentration of 5-MTU. Green-emitting Au/Ag (g) NCs were acquired by mixing the Au/Ag precursors with a small amount of 5-MTU, whereas Au/Ag (r) NCs with an intense red emission were achieved at a high concentration of 5-MTU. Owing to the excellent water-solubility and superior optical properties of the Au/Ag (r) NCs, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive analysis of α-glucosidase was constructed, which was based on the PET mechanism between Au/Ag (r) NCs and PDA as well as inhibition of AA on the self-polymerization process of DA. However, acarbose as an inhibitor of α-glucosidase could effectively suppress the generation of AA, which facilitated the formation of Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA composite and further triggered the ﬂuorescence response of this sensing system. Importantly, the designed ratiometic fluorescent sensor based on the Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA composite was successfully utilized to the identification of α-glucosidase activity in real samples. As a result, the significance of this work can be described as the following distinctive features: (1) Fabrication of Au/Ag (r) NCs/PDA-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor can eﬃciently enhance the accuracy and reproducibility of measurement via monitoring two distinct emission peaks and eliminate the possible variability caused by different types of instruments. (2) The wavelength-tunable Au/Ag NCs are powerful candidates for their applications in the ﬁeld of sensing, bioimaging, light emitting devices, etc. The limitation of this work is that the prepared Au/Ag NCs has relatively high experimental cost. Thus, the pursuit of novel and low-cost method for the preparation of bimetallic nanoclusters (such as Cu/Ag NCs) for the ratiometric fluorescent system is still needed in our future research work.

Mengjun Wang: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Software, Writing - original draft. Xiaobin Zhou: Software. Xiyang Wang: Data curation. Mengke Wang: Formal analysis. Xingguang Su: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
There is no conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775052 and No. 21575048), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130407.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymorphism in atomically precise Cu23 nanocluster incorporating tetrahedral [Cu4](0) Kernel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. L. Han, Z. Liu, L. Feng, Z. Wang, R. K. Gupta, C. M. Aikens, C. H. Tung and D. Sun, Polymorphism in atomically precise Cu23 nanocluster incorporating tetrahedral [Cu4](0) Kernel, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 142 (2020) 5834-5841.

Actuators B Chem. 310 (2020) 127858.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Light-responsive metal-organic framework as an oxidase mimic for cellular glutathione detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, M. Zhou, W. Cao, X. Wang, Q. Wang, S. Li and H. Wei, Light-responsive metal-organic framework as an oxidase mimic for cellular glutathione detection, Anal. Chem. 91(2019) 8170-8175.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mengjun Wang is currently carrying out his Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the synthesis of metal nanoclusters and their applications in bioanalysis.

Xiaobin Zhou is currently carrying out his Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). His research is mainly focused on the synthesis of single atom nanozyme and their applications.

Xiyang Wang is currently a postdoctoral in University of Waterloo. He recieved his MS degree from Jilin University in 2014 and his PhD degree from Jilin University in 2019. He worked as a postdoctoral for 18 months in Jilin University. His research focuses on the controllable synthesis, surface and interface regulation, and applications of transition metal oxides.

Mengke Wang is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the metal nanoclusters and quantum dots and their applications for biosensors.

Xingguang Su is a professor at the Department of Analytical Chemistry at the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. She received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 1992 and her PhD degree from Jilin University (China) in 1999. Her research focuses on the synthesis, characterization, functionalization and application of quantum dots and quantum dots-tagged microspheres in biomedicine.
